href="https://factory.strongdm.ai/techniques/dtu" style="text-decoration: none;"><div class="glass-card" style="padding: 24px; cursor: pointer; position: relative; overflow: hidden; height: 100%; opacity: 1; transform: none;"><div style="position: absolute; top: 0px; left: 0px; right: 0px; height: 3px; background: linear-gradient(90deg, var(--accent), transparent);"></div><div style="width: 48px; height: 48px; border-radius: 12px; background: var(--accent)15; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; color: var(--accent); margin-bottom: 16px;"><svg width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><rect x="9" y="9" width="13" height="13" rx="2" ry="2"></rect><path d="M5 15H4a2 2 0 0 1-2-2V4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h9a2 2 0 0 1 2 2v1"></path></svg></div><h3 style="margin-bottom: 12px; font-size: 18px;">Digital Twin Universe (DTU)</h3><p style="margin: 0px; font-size: 14px; line-height: 1.6;">Clone the externally observable behaviors of critical third-party dependencies. Validate at volumes and rates far exceeding production limits, with deterministic, replayable test conditions.</p><div style="position: absolute; bottom: 20px; right: 20px; color: var(--accent); opacity: 0.5;"><svg width="20" height="20" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><path d="M5 12h14M12 5l7 7-7 7"></path></svg></div></div></a><a href="https://factory.strongdm.ai/techniques/gene-transfusion" style="text-decoration: none;"><div class="glass-card" style="padding: 24px; cursor: pointer; position: relative; overflow: hidden; height: 100%; opacity: 1; transform: none;"><div style="position: absolute; top: 0px; left: 0px; right: 0px; height: 3px; background: linear-gradient(90deg, var(--accent-secondary), transparent);"></div><div style="width: 48px; height: 48px; border-radius: 12px; background: var(--accent-secondary)15; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; color: var(--accent-secondary); margin-bottom: 16px;"><svg width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><line x1="6" y1="3" x2="6" y2="15"></line><circle cx="18" cy="6" r="3"></circle><circle cx="6" cy="18" r="3"></circle><path d="M18 9a9 9 0 0 1-9 9"></path></svg></div><h3 style="margin-bottom: 12px; font-size: 18px;">Gene Transfusion</h3><p style="margin: 0px; font-size: 14px; line-height: 1.6;">Move working patterns between codebases by pointing agents at concrete exemplars. A solution paired with a good reference can be reproduced in new contexts.</p><div style="position: absolute; bottom: 20px; right: 20px; color: var(--accent-secondary); opacity: 0.5;"><svg width="20" height="20" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><path d="M5 12h14M12 5l7 7-7 7"></path></svg></div></div></a><a href="https://factory.strongdm.ai/techniques/filesystem" style="text-decoration: none;"><div class="glass-card" style="padding: 24px; cursor: pointer; position: relative; overflow: hidden; height: 100%; opacity: 1; transform: none;"><div style="position: absolute; top: 0px; left: 0px; right: 0px; height: 3px; background: linear-gradient(90deg, var(--gradient-3), transparent);"></div><div style="width: 48px; height: 48px; border-radius: 12px; background: var(--gradient-3)15; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; color: var(--gradient-3); margin-bottom: 16px;"><svg width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><path d="M22 19a2 2 0 0 1-2 2H4a2 2 0 0 1-2-2V5a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h5l2 3h9a2 2 0 0 1 2 2z"></path></svg></div><h3 style="margin-bottom: 12px; font-size: 18px;">The Filesystem</h3><p style="margin: 0px; font-size: 14px; line-height: 1.6;">Models can navigate repositories quickly and adjust their own context by reading and writing files. Directories, indexes, and on-disk state become a practical memory substrate.</p><div style="position: absolute; bottom: 20px; right: 20px; color: var(--gradient-3); opacity: 0.5;"><svg width="20" height="20" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><path d="M5 12h14M12 5l7 7-7 7"></path></svg></div></div></a><a href="https://factory.strongdm.ai/techniques/shift-work" style="text-decoration: none;"><div class="glass-card" style="padding: 24px; cursor: pointer; position: relative; overflow: hidden; height: 100%; opacity: 1; transform: none;"><div style="position: absolute; top: 0px; left: 0px; right: 0px; height: 3px; background: linear-gradient(90deg, var(--gradient-4), transparent);"></div><div style="width: 48px; height: 48px; border-radius: 12px; background: var(--gradient-4)15; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; color: var(--gradient-4); margin-bottom: 16px;"><svg width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><path d="M9.5 2A2.5 2.5 0 0 1 12 4.5v15a2.5 2.5 0 0 1-4.96.44 2.5 2.5 0 0 1-2.96-3.08 3 3 0 0 1-.34-5.58 2.5 2.5 0 0 1 1.32-4.24 2.5 2.5 0 0 1 4.44-1.54"></path><path d="M14.5 2A2.5 2.5 0 0 0 12 4.5v15a2.5 2.5 0 0 0 4.96.44 2.5 2.5 0 0 0 2.96-3.08 3 3 0 0 0 .34-5.58 2.5 2.5 0 0 0-1.32-4.24 2.5 2.5 0 0 0-4.44-1.54"></path></svg></div><h3 style="margin-bottom: 12px; font-size: 18px;">Shift Work</h3><p style="margin: 0px; font-size: 14px; line-height: 1.6;">Separate interactive work from fully specified work. When intent is complete (specs, tests, existing apps), an agent can run end-to-end without back-and-forth.</p><div style="position: absolute; bottom: 20px; right: 20px; color: var(--gradient-4); opacity: 0.5;"><svg width="20" height="20" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><path d="M5 12h14M12 5l7 7-7 7"></path></svg></div></div></a><a href="https://factory.strongdm.ai/techniques/semport" style="text-decoration: none;"><div class="glass-card" style="padding: 24px; cursor: pointer; position: relative; overflow: hidden; height: 100%; opacity: 1; transform: none;"><div style="position: absolute; top: 0px; left: 0px; right: 0px; height: 3px; background: linear-gradient(90deg, var(--accent), transparent);"></div><div style="width: 48px; height: 48px; border-radius: 12px; background: var(--accent)15; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; color: var(--accent); margin-bottom: 16px;"><svg width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><path d="M8 3L4 7l4 4"></path><path d="M4 7h16"></path><path d="M16 21l4-4-4-4"></path><path d="M20 17H4"></path></svg></div><h3 style="margin-bottom: 12px; font-size: 18px;">Semport</h3><p style="margin: 0px; font-size: 14px; line-height: 1.6;">Semantically-aware automated ports, one time or ongoing. Move code between languages or frameworks while preserving intent.</p><div style="position: absolute; bottom: 20px; right: 20px; color: var(--accent); opacity: 0.5;"><svg width="20" height="20" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><path d="M5 12h14M12 5l7 7-7 7"></path></svg></div></div></a><a href="https://factory.strongdm.ai/techniques/pyramid-summaries" style="text-decoration: none;"><div class="glass-card" style="padding: 24px; cursor: pointer; position: relative; overflow: hidden; height: 100%; opacity: 1; transform: none;"><div style="position: absolute; top: 0px; left: 0px; right: 0px; height: 3px; background: linear-gradient(90deg, var(--accent-secondary), transparent);"></div><div style="width: 48px; height: 48px; border-radius: 12px; background: var(--accent-secondary)15; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; color: var(--accent-secondary); margin-bottom: 16px;"><svg width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><polygon points="12 2 2 7 12 12 22 7 12 2"></polygon><polyline points="2 17 12 22 22 17"></polyline><polyline points="2 12 12 17 22 12"></polyline></svg></div><h3 style="margin-bottom: 12px; font-size: 18px;">Pyramid Summaries</h3><p style="margin: 0px; font-size: 14px; line-height: 1.6;">Reversible summarization at multiple zoom levels. Compress context without losing the ability to expand back to full detail.</p><div style="position: absolute; bottom: 20px; right: 20px; color: var(--accent-secondary); opacity: 0.5;"><svg width="20" height="20" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><path d="M5 12h14M12 5l7 7-7 7"></path></svg></div></div></a></div><section class="glass-card" style="padding: 32px; margin-bottom: 80px; opacity: 1; transform: none;"><h2 style="margin-bottom: 24px;">The Validation Constraint</h2><p style="font-size: 16px; line-height: 1.8; margin-bottom: 20px;">Given zero hand-written code and zero traditional review, we required a system that could:</p><ul style="font-size: 16px; line-height: 1.8; padding-left: 24px; margin-bottom: 20px;"><li style="margin-bottom: 8px;">Grow from cascades of natural-language specifications</li><li>Be validated automatically without semantic inspection of source</li></ul><p style="font-size: 16px; line-height: 1.8; margin: 0px;">Code was treated analogously to an ML model snapshot: <span style="color: var(--accent); font-weight: 600;">opaque weights</span> whose correctness is inferred exclusively from externally observable behavior. Internal structure is treated as opaque.</p></section><div class="jsx-f1f6f65db6fe5050 bottom-nav"><a class="btn-secondary" href="https://factory.strongdm.ai/principles">← Principles</a><a class="btn-secondary" href="https://factory.strongdm.ai/products">Products →</a></div></div></main></div><script src="./Pyramid Summaries _ StrongDM Software Factory_files/webpack-e82a1bd3e1741532.js" class="__className_f367f3"><div style="position:fixed;inset:0;z-index:-10;background:#05080f"></div><div class="page-shell"><main><nav class="jsx-a4a3b0bebcf6a68e nav-bar"><a class="nav-brand" menu" class="jsx-a4a3b0bebcf6a68e nav-hamburger"><span class="jsx-a4a3b0bebcf6a68e hamburger-line "></span><span class="jsx-a4a3b0bebcf6a68e hamburger-line "></span><span class="jsx-a4a3b0bebcf6a68e hamburger-line "></span></button></nav><div style="max-width: 800px; margin: 0px auto; padding: 0px 24px;"><div style="text-align: center; margin-bottom: 60px; padding-top: 120px;"><div style="display: inline-block; font-size: 11px; font-weight: 600; letter-spacing: 2px; color: var(--gradient-3); margin-bottom: 16px; text-transform: uppercase; padding: 6px 12px; background: var(--gradient-3)15; border-radius: 20px; opacity: 1; transform: none;">Technique</div><h1 class="gradient-text" style="margin-bottom: 20px; opacity: 1; transform: none;">The Filesystem</h1><p style="max-width: 700px; margin: 0px auto; font-size: 18px; line-height: 1.7; opacity: 1; transform: none;">Mutable, inspectable world-state. Agents navigate and contour their own context.</p></div><article style="margin-bottom: 80px;"><div style="opacity: 1; transform: none;"><p style="font-size: 18px; line-height: 1.8; margin-bottom: 24px;">Early on, we observed: agents reliably build an on-disk index, write scratch state, and rehydrate context via <code style="background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.1); padding: 2px 6px; border-radius: 4px;">rg</code>/open.</p><div class="glass-card" style="padding: 24px; margin-bottom: 32px; border-left: 4px solid var(--gradient-3);"><h4 style="margin-bottom: 12px; color: var(--gradient-3);">The Thought Experiment</h4><p style="margin: 0px; font-size: 15px; line-height: 1.7; font-style: italic;">If all you had were <code style="background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.1); padding: 2px 6px; border-radius: 4px;">$HOME</code> and <code style="background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.1); padding: 2px 6px; border-radius: 4px;">vi</code>, how would you keep track of an email you just sent, a grocery list, a Slack conversation, or compatibility constraints such as tire sizes versus rims?</p></div><h2 style="margin-top: 48px; margin-bottom: 24px;">The Answer</h2><p style="font-size: 16px; line-height: 1.8; margin-bottom: 24px;">The answer is straightforward:</p><div style="display: grid; gap: 16px; margin-bottom: 32px;"><div class="glass-card" style="padding: 20px; display: flex; gap: 16px; align-items: center; opacity: 1; transform: none;"><div style="font-size: 24px;">📁</div><div style="font-weight: 500;">Create directories with meaningful names</div></div><div class="glass-card" style="padding: 20px; display: flex; gap: 16px; align-items: center; opacity: 1; transform: none;"><div style="font-size: 24px;">📋</div><div style="font-weight: 500;">Create indexes (typically Markdown)</div></div><div class="glass-card" style="padding: 20px; display: flex; gap: 16px; align-items: center; opacity: 1; transform: none;"><div style="font-size: 24px;">💾</div><div style="font-weight: 500;">Write state to disk</div></div></div><p style="font-size: 16px; line-height: 1.8; margin-bottom: 24px;">In practice this state is most often <strong style="color: var(--text);">Markdown</strong>, but frequently <strong style="color: var(--text);">JSON</strong> or <strong style="color: var(--text);">YAML</strong>, and in specific circumstances <strong style="color: var(--text);">XML</strong>.</p><h2 style="margin-top: 48px; margin-bottom: 24px;">Genrefying</h2><p style="font-size: 16px; line-height: 1.8; margin-bottom: 24px;">As the hierarchy of concepts evolves, it inevitably becomes unbalanced, redundant, or confusing. At that point, the corrective action is the same as for a human operator:<strong style="color: var(--accent);"> reorganize</strong>.</p><blockquote style="margin: 32px 0px; padding: 24px 32px; border-left: 4px solid var(--accent); background: rgba(245, 166, 35, 0.05); border-radius: 0px 12px 12px 0px; position: relative; opacity: 0; transform: translateX(-20px);"><div style="position: absolute; top: -10px; left: 20px; font-size: 60px; font-family: Georgia, serif; color: var(--accent); opacity: 0.3; line-height: 1;">"</div><div style="font-size: 18px; font-style: italic; color: var(--text); line-height: 1.6;">In library science this process is called <em>genrefying</em>: restructuring information to optimize future retrieval.</div></blockquote><p style="font-size: 16px; line-height: 1.8; margin-bottom: 24px;">Functionally, this resembles a familiar data-structures operation: <strong style="color: var(--text);">rebalancing and reindexing</strong>. Here it is mediated by LLMs operating directly over the filesystem.</p><h2 style="margin-top: 48px; margin-bottom: 24px;">Implications</h2><div class="glass-card" style="padding: 32px; margin-bottom: 32px;"><div style="display: grid; gap: 24px;"><div><div style="font-weight: 600; margin-bottom: 8px; color: var(--gradient-3);">Self-Organizing Context</div><div style="font-size: 14px; color: var(--muted);">Agents can create and maintain their own knowledge structures as they work</div></div><div><div style="font-weight: 600; margin-bottom: 8px; color: var(--gradient-3);">Persistent Memory</div><div style="font-size: 14px; color: var(--muted);">State survives across sessions. This makes long-running work practical across many model calls.</div></div><div><div style="font-weight: 600; margin-bottom: 8px; color: var(--gradient-3);">Inspectable State</div><div style="font-size: 14px; color: var(--muted);">Humans can audit, modify, or reset agent state at any time</div></div><div><div style="font-weight: 600; margin-bottom: 8px; color: var(--gradient-3);">Composable Knowledge</div><div style="font-size: 14px; color: var(--muted);">Multiple agents can share and build on the same filesystem state</div></div></div></div><p style="font-size: 16px; line-height: 1.8;">The filesystem becomes a substrate for agent memory, state, and coordination. It is both an artifact store and a <strong style="color: var(--text);">mutable, inspectable world-state</strong>.</p></div></article><div class="jsx-f1f6f65db6fe5050 bottom-nav"><a class="btn-secondary" href="https://factory.strongdm.ai/techniques/gene-transfusion">← Gene Transfusion</a><a class="btn-secondary" href="https://factory.strongdm.ai/techniques/shift-work">Shift Work →</a></div></div></main></div><script src="./The Filesystem _ StrongDM Software Factory_files/webpack-e82a1bd3e1741532.js"
```text
# Pyramid Summaries Technique

**Core Concept:**
Pyramid Summaries enable reversible multi-level summarization, allowing agents to compress context while maintaining the ability to expand back to complete details. This approach mirrors multi-resolution image formats and map-tiling systems.

**Key Principle:**
"Summarize this bug report in 2 words. Now 4. Now 8. Now 16." Each tier preserves essential meaning while adjusting detail granularity.

**Primary Benefits:**
- **Rapid enumeration** with reduced context displacement
- Agents can survey hundreds of items at compressed levels, then selectively expand interesting ones
- Finite context windows are used more efficiently

**Implementation Pattern:**
The technique pairs effectively with MapReduce and clustering:
1. **Map:** Generate pyramid summaries for items in parallel
2. **Cluster:** Group related items using compressed representations
3. **Reduce:** Synthesize insights across clusters, expanding detail as needed

**Practical Analogy:**
The pattern mirrors executive decision-making—beginning with high-level organizational views, drilling into departments, then teams, and finally individuals, expanding detail only where signals demand it.

**Core Insight:**
"Context windows are finite. Attention is precious. Pyramid Summaries let you see the forest and the trees, just not all at once."
```

```text
     1→# Memory Management — Future Exploration
     2→
     3→**Created:** 2026-02-27
     4→**Status:** Backlog — investigate when current setup shows limitations
     5→
     6→---
     7→
     8→## Investigate: RAG-based Memory with Vector DB
     9→
    10→**What:** Use a vector database (e.g., Supabase pgvector, Chroma, Pinecone) to store and retrieve project knowledge via semantic search instead of flat file loading.
    11→
    12→**Why interesting:** Current system relies on keyword grep and manual file reading. A vector DB would allow "find me everything related to webhook retry logic" to surface relevant ADRs, code comments, and past decisions without knowing exact file locations.
    13→
    14→**Concerns:** Infrastructure overhead, embedding cost, keeping embeddings in sync with source of truth. May be solving a problem `/tribal-knowledge` already handles well enough.
    15→
    16→---
    17→
    18→## Investigate: Graph Database for Entity Relationships
    19→
    20→**What:** Use a graph DB (e.g., Neo4j, Supabase + pg_graphql) to model service relationships, ownership, dependencies, and decision chains as a queryable graph.
    21→
    22→**Why interesting:** Research mentions Mem0/Zep-style knowledge graphs for "relational memory." Would enable queries like "what services are affected if we change the auth token format?" that flat files can't answer efficiently.
    23→
    24→**Concerns:** Heavy setup, another system to maintain. The workspace-map.yaml + agent-os specs may cover 80% of this with zero infrastructure.
    25→
    26→---
    27→
    28→## Investigate: MCP Memory Servers
    29→
    30→**What:** Use existing MCP servers (e.g., `@anthropic/memory-server`, community MCP tools) to give Claude persistent memory via tool calls rather than file-based memory.
    31→
    32→**Why interesting:** MCP tools integrate natively with Claude Code. Could provide structured memory operations (save, query, update, delete) without custom skills. Some MCP memory servers support semantic search.
    33→
    34→**Concerns:** Another dependency. Need to evaluate whether it provides meaningful capability beyond what CLAUDE.md + on-demand context files already do. Vendor lock-in risk.
    35→
    36→---
    37→
    38→## Investigate: Supabase as Unified Memory Backend
    39→
    40→**What:** People report using Supabase as a combined vector store + graph + structured data backend for AI agent memory. Supports pgvector for embeddings, row-level security, real-time subscriptions.
    41→
    42→**Why interesting:** Single infrastructure piece for multiple memory needs. Could serve as both the vector DB and the graph DB from the items above.
    43→
    44→**Concerns:** Overkill for current scale. Worth investigating only if flat-file memory proves insufficient after 2-3 months of use.
    45→
    46→---
    47→

    49→
    50→**What:** If Claude Code adds the ability for spawned agents to get their own project scope (feature request identified in this session), re-evaluate whether platform auto-memory becomes useful.
    51→

    60→1. MCP Memory Servers — lowest effort to test, native integration
    61→2. Supabase pgvector — if MCP servers feel limited
```
